text: What is a chiral carbon?
choices: A carbon atom bonded to four different atoms or groups
A carbon atom double-bonded to oxygen
A carbon atom found only in lipids
A carbon atom that has lost all electrons
explain: A chiral carbon is bonded to four different substituents and can give rise to stereoisomers.

text: What does complementary base pairing mean?
choices: DNA bases pair randomly as long as the strand stays stable
Specific nitrogenous bases pair with one another according to consistent rules
Any purine can pair with any purine
Bases pair only in RNA, not DNA
explain: Complementary base pairing follows specific rules (A–T, C–G in DNA), ensuring accurate pairing.

text: What is the main role of a disaccharide?
choices: It is always a structural polymer in cell walls
It is composed of two monosaccharides joined by a glycosidic bond
It is a protein made of two amino acids
It is a nucleotide containing two phosphate groups
explain: A disaccharide consists of two sugar monomers linked by a glycosidic bond.

text: Which statement best describes an enzyme’s active site?
choices: It is the region of the enzyme where the substrate binds and the reaction is facilitated
It is the phosphate backbone of the enzyme
It is the portion of the enzyme made entirely of lipids
It is a region found only in denatured enzymes
explain: The active site binds substrate and provides the specific chemical environment for catalysis.

text: What is the main difference between DNA and RNA sugars?
choices: DNA contains glucose, while RNA contains fructose
DNA contains deoxyribose, while RNA contains ribose
DNA contains starch, while RNA contains glycogen
DNA contains amino acids, while RNA contains nucleotides
explain: DNA uses deoxyribose and RNA uses ribose as the pentose sugar in their backbones.

text: Which intermolecular force is especially important in holding complementary DNA strands together?
choices: Peptide bonds
Hydrogen bonds
Disulfide bonds
Glycosidic linkages
explain: Hydrogen bonds between complementary bases stabilize the DNA double helix.

text: A scientist isolates two amino acids that differ only in the properties of their R groups. One amino acid has a charged side chain, and the other has a nonpolar side chain. Why can this difference strongly affect protein structure?
choices: R-group properties influence how amino acids interact with water and with each other during folding
All amino acids behave identically once incorporated into a protein
Charged amino acids cannot be part of proteins
Nonpolar amino acids automatically break peptide bonds
explain: Side-chain chemistry drives folding and interactions; charge and polarity affect tertiary structure.

text: A carbohydrate is hydrolyzed into identical monomers. The monomers are then reassembled into a different polysaccharide with different biological properties. Which statement best explains this outcome?
choices: The same monomers can form different polymers depending on how they are linked
Reassembled monomers always produce the original polymer
Hydrolysis changes glucose into amino acids
All polysaccharides have identical structures and functions
explain: Linkage pattern and orientation determine polysaccharide structure and function.

text: A mutation changes one nucleotide in DNA, resulting in a different amino acid in the final protein. The new amino acid has a sulfhydryl group, and the original did not. Which prediction is most reasonable?
choices: The protein may form a new disulfide bond that changes its stability or shape
The protein must lose all secondary structure
The mutation will necessarily prevent transcription of the gene
Sulfhydryl groups are found only in nucleic acids, not proteins
explain: Introducing a cysteine can enable new disulfide bonds, altering stability or conformation.

text: A biologist compares two nucleic acid molecules. Molecule A contains thymine and is more chemically stable for long-term storage. Molecule B contains uracil and participates more directly in short-term information transfer. Which conclusion is best?
choices: Molecule A is DNA and Molecule B is RNA
Molecule A is a protein and Molecule B is a lipid
Molecule A and B are both carbohydrates
Molecule B is DNA because uracil stabilizes long-term information storage
explain: DNA (thymine) is typically the stable storage molecule; RNA (uracil) is often involved in transient information transfer.

text: A solution of an unknown biomolecule remains mixed uniformly in water. Chemical analysis shows that the molecule contains many hydroxyl groups and no long hydrocarbon tails. Which conclusion is most likely?
choices: The molecule is likely polar and hydrophilic
The molecule is likely a triglyceride
The molecule must be nonpolar
The molecule cannot form hydrogen bonds with water
explain: Hydroxyl groups promote polarity and hydrogen bonding, making molecules water-soluble.

text: A researcher observes that a membrane phospholipid has one saturated fatty acid tail and one unsaturated fatty acid tail. Why might this matter for membrane behavior?
choices: The unsaturated tail introduces a bend that can affect packing and fluidity
The saturated tail becomes positively charged in water
The phospholipid can no longer interact with water
The molecule stops being a lipid when one tail is unsaturated
explain: Unsaturated tails create kinks that reduce tight packing and increase membrane fluidity.

text: A scientist replaces several polar amino acids on the surface of a cytoplasmic enzyme with nonpolar amino acids. Which result is most likely?
choices: The enzyme may become less soluble or fold less favorably in the aqueous cytoplasm
The enzyme will automatically become embedded in DNA
The enzyme will definitely gain activity
The enzyme will lose all amino acids except the altered ones
explain: Reducing surface polarity decreases favorable interactions with water and can reduce solubility or stability.

text: A student says, “Because DNA strands are held together by hydrogen bonds, the strands should be permanently fixed and impossible to separate.” Which response is best?
choices: The student is incorrect because hydrogen bonds are individually weak enough to allow strand separation when conditions require it
The student is correct because hydrogen bonds are stronger than covalent bonds
The student is incorrect because DNA strands are linked by peptide bonds, not hydrogen bonds
The student is correct because DNA never separates in cells
explain: Hydrogen bonds are numerous and stabilizing but can be broken during replication or transcription when needed.

text: A newly discovered molecule is made from monomers, contains a sugar-phosphate backbone, and stores hereditary information. Which statement is best supported?
choices: The molecule is a nucleic acid, and its monomer is a nucleotide
The molecule is a protein, and its monomer is an amino acid
The molecule is a lipid, and its monomer is a fatty acid
The molecule is a carbohydrate, and its monomer is a monosaccharide
explain: A sugar-phosphate backbone and information storage are characteristic of nucleic acids (DNA/RNA).

text:
Students test four unknown samples.

Sample W:
Positive Benedict’s test
Negative Biuret test
Negative iodine test

Sample X:
Negative Benedict’s test
Positive Biuret test
Negative iodine test

Sample Y:
Negative Benedict’s test
Negative Biuret test
Positive iodine test

Sample Z:
Negative Benedict’s test
Negative Biuret test
Separates from water and leaves a translucent spot on paper

Which identification is most likely?
choices: W = reducing sugar, X = protein, Y = starch, Z = lipid
W = protein, X = starch, Y = lipid, Z = reducing sugar
W = lipid, X = reducing sugar, Y = protein, Z = starch
W = nucleic acid, X = water, Y = amino acid, Z = cellulose
explain: Indicator patterns match reducing sugar (Benedict), protein (Biuret), starch (iodine), and lipid (translucent spot).

text:
A lab group measures enzyme activity at different pH values.

pH 4: low activity
pH 6: moderate activity
pH 7.5: highest activity
pH 10: low activity

Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: The enzyme has an optimal pH near 7.5, and changes in pH likely alter interactions important for activity
The enzyme works better as pH continuously increases
The enzyme must be a lipid because it is affected by pH
pH has no effect on the structure of biological molecules
explain: Enzymes often have narrow pH optima because ionization states affect structure and catalysis.

text: Researchers compare two proteins that differ by one amino acid. Protein A functions normally. Protein B contains an amino acid with a bulky nonpolar side chain במקום a smaller polar side chain in the active site region and binds the substrate poorly. Which explanation is best?
choices: The substitution likely changed the shape or chemistry of the active site, reducing substrate binding
Protein B must have lost all peptide bonds
Protein A is actually RNA because it binds a substrate
Protein B must be longer than Protein A
explain: A bulky substitution in the active site can sterically or chemically disrupt substrate binding.

text: A scientist compares two DNA molecules of the same length. Molecule 1 has 65% G-C base pairs, while Molecule 2 has 35% G-C base pairs. When heated gradually, Molecule 1 separates into single strands at a higher temperature. Which explanation best fits the data?
choices: G-C pairs contribute more hydrogen bonds per pair, increasing DNA stability
Molecule 1 must contain ribose instead of deoxyribose
Molecule 2 contains no covalent bonds
Heating affects only RNA, not DNA
explain: Higher G-C content raises melting temperature because G-C pairs have three hydrogen bonds vs two for A-T.

text:
Students compare the effect of temperature on two liquids after identical heating.

Liquid A: temperature increases by 6°C
Liquid B: temperature increases by 15°C

Which statement is best supported?
choices: Liquid A likely has a higher specific heat than Liquid B
Liquid B absorbed no energy
Liquid A must be basic
Liquid B must contain more hydrogen bonds than Liquid A
explain: A smaller temperature change under equal heating suggests a higher specific heat capacity.

text: A mutation changes one codon in a gene. The new amino acid introduces a positive charge where a negative charge was previously required for binding another molecule. The protein still folds but interacts weakly with its partner. Which interpretation is most reasonable?
choices: The mutation likely disrupted an ionic or charge-based interaction important for function
The mutation must have shortened the protein
The mutation proves amino acid properties do not matter
The protein has become a carbohydrate
explain: Reversing charge at an interaction interface can disrupt electrostatic binding while leaving overall fold intact.

text: A biologist places phospholipids and triglycerides into separate beakers of water. The phospholipids form organized bilayer-like structures, but the triglycerides form droplets. Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: Phospholipids are amphipathic, while triglycerides are mostly nonpolar
Triglycerides are made of amino acids, so they cannot form droplets
Phospholipids contain DNA bases that guide bilayer formation
Water becomes nonpolar in the presence of phospholipids
explain: Amphipathic phospholipids self-assemble into bilayers; triglycerides are largely hydrophobic and form droplets.

text: Researchers identify a molecule that is built from amino acid monomers and contains multiple disulfide bonds. Under reducing conditions, those bonds break and the molecule becomes less stable. Which conclusion is most supported?
choices: Disulfide bonds contribute to stabilization of higher-order protein structure
Disulfide bonds are part of nucleic acid base pairing
The molecule must be a polysaccharide
Reducing conditions remove all peptide bonds from the molecule
explain: Breaking disulfide bonds reduces covalent crosslinks that stabilize tertiary/quaternary structure.

text: A teacher gives students two carbohydrate polymers. Both are made entirely of glucose. Polymer A is readily hydrolyzed by a human digestive enzyme. Polymer B is not. Which explanation is best?
choices: The two polymers likely differ in glycosidic linkage orientation or branching pattern
Polymer B must actually be a lipid
Polymer A contains amino acids mixed into the chain
Human digestive enzymes hydrolyze all glucose polymers equally
explain: Enzymes recognize specific linkages and structures; different linkage types affect digestibility.

text: A student analyzes an unknown biomolecule and finds that it contains carbon, hydrogen, oxygen, and nitrogen; it is made from monomers with amino and carboxyl groups; and a change in one monomer alters its folding. Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: The molecule is a protein whose function depends on amino acid sequence and resulting shape
The molecule is DNA because DNA folding depends on amino acids
The molecule is a phospholipid because phospholipids contain carboxyl groups in amino acid monomers
The molecule is starch because starch monomers have amino groups
explain: Amino-acid-like monomers and folding dependence identify the molecule as a protein.

text: A researcher studies two related enzymes. Enzyme X has many polar residues exposed on its surface and functions in the cytoplasm. Enzyme Y has many nonpolar residues exposed on its surface and is embedded in a membrane. Which idea does this best illustrate?
choices: The environment helps determine which molecular structures are favored for proper function
All enzymes have identical external amino acid properties
Membrane proteins are made of nucleotides rather than amino acids
Cytoplasmic proteins cannot contain nonpolar amino acids at all
explain: Surface properties matching the environment support stability and function (cytoplasm vs membrane).
